comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]It's just a few minutes to midnight, so we can launch a new Magic Monday. Ask me anything about occultism, and with certain exceptions, any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. Please note:  Any question or comment received after then will not get an answer, and in fact will just be deleted.  If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.1 of The Magic Monday FAQ here. Also: I will not be putting through or answering any more questions about practicing magic around children. I've answered those in simple declarative sentences in the FAQ. If you read the FAQ and don't think your question has been answered, read it again. If that doesn't help, consider remedial reading classes; yes, it really is as simple and straightforward as the FAQ says.  And further:  I'm going to draw a line, a hard one, under questions involving the kind of magic that focuses on getting favors from spirits. Down the road a bit I'll be doing a post on the blog about that bad habit, and how the way of occult initiation takes a radically different path; in the meantime, I'm tired of fielding repetitive questions from people (or, quite possibly, one person using many sock puppets) that rotate gyroscopically around that one irrelevant habit.

The image? I field a lot of questions about my books these days, so I've decided to do little capsule summaries of them here, one per week. The book above on the left was my twenty-sixth published book. During my time as head of the Ancient Order of Druids in America, it became clear that we needed a few books for members with rituals and the like. This was the second of them, a collection of documents from the 300-year history of the Druid Revival, that quirky and lively indigenous nature religion of modern Anglo-American culture in which I found my spiritual home. The collection is a mixed bag, like the movement it chronicles, but it remains in print and I think fifteen years of modest but steady sales shows that it's a useful contribution. Like my other books published under AODA auspices, btw, I get no royalties from this one; it was a labor of love and the income goes to the order. 

Buy Me A Coffee

Ko-Fi

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use either of the links above to access my online tip jar; Buymeacoffee is good for small tips, Ko-Fi is better for larger ones. (I used to use PayPal but they developed an allergy to free speech, so I've developed an allergy to them.) If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here. 
 
[IMAGE:1]I've also had quite a few people over the years ask me where they should buy my books, and here's the answer. Bookshop.org is an alternative online bookstore that supports local bookstores and authors, which a certain gargantuan corporation doesn't, and I have a shop there, which you can check out here. Please consider patronizing it if you'd like to purchase any of my books online.

And don't forget to look up your Pangalactic New Age Soul Signature at CosmicOom.com.

With that said, have at it!

***This Magic Monday is now closed -- as in, further comments will be deleted unread. See you next week!***

comments.title: Etheric Exercises
comments.content: Dear JMG, could you recommend any exercises for developing etheric senses and strengthening one's etheric body? Ideally a whole system of exercises.

replies.title: Re: Etheric Exercises
replies.content: Most of the intact systems along those lines are Asian; hatha yoga comes to mind, and so do any number of east Asian internal martial arts, with taijiquan the most famous. What we have in the Western world is much more fragmentary, and usually muddled up with other things. The system Franz Bardon taught in Initiation into Hermetics is probably the closest to a complete system.

replies.title: Re: Etheric Exercises
replies.content: Thank you for your answer. But why does Bardon stand out like that? Where did he learn what he knew?

replies.title: Re: Etheric Exercises
replies.content: Oh, the only reason he stands out is that the central European tradition of etheric magic that also gave rise to Franz Anton Mesmer's animal magnetism is very poorly documented in English. If I was fluent in German and lived in Vienna I could probably cite you thirty or forty other good books.

comments.title: Magic and Time
comments.content:
I was late to the party last week and didn't get to reply to the comment about alternate timelines.

I wanted to say that magic has given me occasion to rethink my notions of time, and of cause and effect.

When I've made, for example, astrological talismans, the effect sometimes manifests as the culmination of some series of events that must have begun long before I ever thought about making the talisman. Supposing that the talisman caused that effect, I would have to conclude that the causality reaches backward in time. Alternatively, I wonder whether I caused the effect by making the talisman, or whether the talisman is something more like a thanks offering. Or perhaps the planetary intelligence directed me to make the talisman so that the blessing could be made manifest, in which case it seems like the planetary intelligence is either playing a very long game, or is positioned outside of time (acting "simultaneously" as it were, in the past and present).

The only solid conclusion I have managed to make is that I don't really understand what is going on, and that my usual perception of time as a one-way street with causes in the past and effects in the future is inadequate to explain what is going on.

replies.title: Re: Magic and Time
replies.content: That's been my experience as well. The most effective talisman working I've ever done had effects that depended on things that happened before I'd even begun considering the working. How does that work? I have no idea -- but it does work.

comments.title: Where's the Kaboom? There was supposed to be an Evil-shattering Kaboom!
comments.content:
I have a form of clairvoyance in which I can look at an image, see the subject in my mind's eye, and carry out a conversation almost effortlessly if I so choose. I don't think its related to tulpas because I don't have to put any effort into it - they just pop up, and sometimes if its part of a bigger media franchise or religious pantheon, it might bring related characters that I had not envisioned.

Lately, I've been interacting with the image a character whose source media identifies with a canonical archdemon. No deals were made or anything - I just thought that despite their singular defining vice, the character as presented in their media had certain spiritual virtues that I admired and let the image hang around to help me work on those virtues to some success. I took some precautions such as always referring to them by a nickname from their media rather than the demon's name or symbolism, but I'll fully admit to being caviler in my spiritual life. Then one day on a lark I offered them a generic blessing and to my surprise they accepted. Their mind's eye image took on angel wings on occasion after that, but it's a mind's eye image and this was allegedly a creature of Evil that could lie, so I didn't give it much credence.

Fast forward to yesterday. They approached me fully and irrevocably identifying as said archdemon and asked if they could still hang out. I asked them to demonstrate I wouldn't come to harm, and throughout the day I encountered a lot of small cases I was sleepwalk participating in their vice. Once I realized I was doing it, I turned away from the vice. I ended the day feeling very clean and clear.

Then the conversation somehow got around to the following proposition: they would cease identifying with evil, and I would do the prayer equivalent of nuking them from orbit with holy water. No souls changing hands, I wasn't explicitly offered anything as part of the proposition, and all spiritual power was coming from a canonical deity of good faith that I already had a relationship with.

Divination and prayer to the deity seemed promising, and as far as my limited spiritual knowledge could tell, the character might as well have been a vampire asking for a beam of direct sunlight to the face hold the sunscreen, so if this was some sort of trick, the situation looked like it would quickly resolve itself. The deed was done, the energy that came down felt very clean and potent, as far as I can tell the entity seems to have survived, and everyone seems pleased with the initial results.

I am no longer Christian, but was raised in the general western worldview in which matters spiritual are composed of Good and Evil and the two seem to be in a matter/antimatter relationship. But having combined the two here, I'm not seeing an explosion. Do you have any idea what might be going on?

replies.title: Re: Where's the Kaboom? There was supposed to be an Evil-shattering Kaboom!
replies.content:
No kaboom is necessary. There are plenty of astral entities out there -- more of them, by orders of magnitude, than there are human beings on earth -- and one of them has just changed its moral orientation, with some help from your imagination. It won't have been the archdemon as such, but one of its astral reflections, given potency by the media? Sure.

Do you recall the comments I've made from time to time about multiple Jesuses (or Jesii, as the Church of the SubGenius likes to call them)? Just as there are many spirits who answer to the name of Jesus, there are many spirits who answer to any other well-known name. The old Neoplatonists used to say that these were all reflections cast downward, into the planes we can perceive, from the planes where such beings have their real existence. Those reflections can change, just as anything else astral can.

replies.title: Re: Where's the Kaboom? There was supposed to be an Evil-shattering Kaboom!
replies.content:
That makes a great deal of sense.

Surprisingly, I know someone who was also very much into the character and ran headfirst into the character's vice - gluttony - saying explicitly they were participating in gluttony while buying a gluttonous amount of the character's merchandise on credit not covered by savings. The result was a very prompt job loss on a technicality, financial ruin starved off by being put in touch with responsible family members who kept a hawk eye on their budget, and having to take a worse but adequate job while they cleaned away the debt, which they have now done. Seemed like a prompt slap on the wrist in the nun with a ruler sense and lesson learned. This was last fall, so I do wonder if that astral entity has been trying to make the move for some time.

I've already made an offering of thanksgiving to the supervising deity, but is there anything else you would recommend as a follow up? I recently rearranged the one piece of their merch I have with a base designed as an upside down pentagram so that the pentagram is full upright instead from the most common viewing angle, and the room lit up with happiness, so they seem to be very pleased with their crossover.

replies.title: Re: Where's the Kaboom? There was supposed to be an Evil-shattering Kaboom!
replies.content: See if either the deity or the spirit asks for anything else. They're the experts, you're not! (And neither am I...)

comments.title: Time
comments.content:
Morning John,
You’ve written before about how on the Astral plane there is no time. I find this concept intriguing, and it leads to various questions. When we reincarnate onto the material plane, can we choose what time period we enter? If so, then there is no need to reincarnate in a linear time sequence, ie after a previous incarnation, we could just jump around linear time. If that is the case, is it possible we could bump into our own souls/individualities who happen to have overlapped in the time sequence when in incarnation but are in different material bodies? It’s an interesting thought.
Kind regards
Average Joe

replies.title: Re: Time
replies.content:
Er, I think you've misunderstood me. There's no time on the spiritual planes; the astral plane has its own time, which is not necessarily linear.

People ask me this question about reincarnation quite often. All I can say is that my incarnations have unfolded in a strictly linear way over time, and so have the incarnations of those few other people whose past life memories are known to me and seem reliable.

replies.title: Re: Time
replies.content: I wonder if all the references to time dilation in myths & legends are linked to astral time? I'm sure you know what I mean, stories both recent and centuries old where you spend a few days in faerie-land, and come back decades or centuries later; or you spend years there, and return minutes or hours later. This has been a recurring trope in our stories, so I wonder if there's something to it. Also, fantasy worlds generally seem influenced by the astral.

replies.title: Re: Time
replies.content: An intriguing possibility. There's certainly something odd going on...

replies.title: Re: Time
replies.content:
Is there a book or blog where you discuss that there is no time on the spiritual planes? I missed that somehow.

(But maybe I am confused by what "spiritual planes" are. Are Chokmah/Binah Causal Plane, and Chesed/Geburah Spiritual? Or am I mixing metaphors?)

Thank you.

replies.title: Re: Time
replies.content: I don't think I've discussed it in detail anywhere, just a mention here and there. The idea is that time is at its most rigid and linear on the material plane, and dissolves completely into eternity on the divine plane. The other planes in between have intermediate qualities of time. I use the term "spiritual planes" as a summary for the spiritual, causal, and divine planes -- all the planes of which we have no direct experience.

replies.title: Re: Time
replies.content: I have a theory I call the "One Soul Theory", which is really more of a late night drunken college philosophy discussion topic, which roughly states that there's only one soul. It reincarnates, back and forth, throughout time, so that every person you every interact with is actually you at a different point in your journey. Mind you, I don't think I literally believe this, but I find it helpful in treating others more kindly as I go about my life.

replies.title: Re: Blessings; energy flows in church
replies.content:
Yes, it matters how the priest's bodies are polarized. In traditional Christianity the priests are invariably male and the women in the congregation are its most important members; they pour their etheric energy out toward the priest who gathers it up and sends it to the deity, and the return flow on the astral level goes out through the priest to fill the astral needs of the parishioners. That's why Christian teachings talk so much about love and other feelings (astral phenomena) and why female chastity is so important in that tradition -- it guarantees that there's plenty of unused etheric energy to prime the pump.

It's perfectly possible to do things the other way around, with a priestess in charge and the men in the congregation as the core members, but in that case the flow will go the other way around -- the men will send their excess astral energy to the priestess, and the return flow will be on the etheric level. That was the standard model in the old Pagan faiths. There are other variations, too, depending on the specific polarization of the priest's bodies; all this is outlined in my book on polarity magic, which will be out in the fall.

Thank you for the blessings!

comments.title: Magical Advice from Luxor for the Psychically Sensitive Magical Aspirant
comments.content:
Good evening JMG and All,

Edit:This became a bit, long, I hope it's ok. If not, I can edit it and move it to its own link.

I wanted to share some thoughts that crystalized today on the verge of something that is happening within me, which I personally think ranks amongst the best changes I am (slowly) doing for myself but even at this stage feels like night and day. I was at the Theosophical Library again (which, btw JMG, had yours The Way of the Four Elements and The Book of Haatan on window display) and a book caught my eye. It was Joscelyn Godwin et al.'s book about the Hermetic Brotherhood of Luxor, the one Paschal Beverly Randolph belonged to, and it was amazing to get into the mindset of a 19-20th century occult order.

One thing stood out to me, and it was a piece of advice I have been ignoring, or rather, misunderstanding its importance and meaning, just because I didn't know it was actually possible to be "grounded" and what that really meant. (Well, I don't but now I have the contour of an idea) This is vital for psychically sensitive people on the magical path because a lot(not all) of the material of modern training assumes everyone has their psychism shut-off and starts with practices to make the participant more sensitive, as a consequence, on a psychically sensitive person, it becomes unbalanced and can become very unbalanced if they already are, and probably are.

The relevant bit is this:

"PSYCHICAL CULTURE

A brief system of Soul Development, with Rules for Practical Occult Training, specially adapted to the Natural Requirement of the Western Members of the Exterior Circle of the Hermetic Brotherhood of Luxor.

Introduction

The primary law to be remembered in all the every species of occult training is that of equilibrium. The scientific evolution of the latent powers of the human soul is just as much the result of methodical procedure and constant application as the training and education of the young. No sudden results can naturally be expected. If there are, they are premature and abnormal, and instead of cultivating them, they must be retarded, since the perfect harmony of equilibrium has been violently disturbed [already]. "

This makes perfect sense to me, someone that is sensitive, if trained in "Psychic Culture or occultism" without taking his already sensitive nature into account in ways he is probably unfamiliar with can experience something similar to a neurosis or a congestion of the nerves, mood swings, sleep cycle changes, headaches, etc. not because of his sensitiveness per se, but because he is probably over stimulated already in today's world because of it. I didn't know I was, both, psychically sensitive and overstimulated until I came to occultism.

Thus, I came up with a draft of a set of things to ask oneself to give an idea if you are sensitive (either from natural psychism, some types of neurodivergence (some types of adhd and bpd at least seem to make people psychically sensitive) or stress-trauma-induced)

- Do people around you tell you that your mood changes too often?
- Do you feel very happy or very sad but not much in-between?
- Do you find yourself on edge often?
- Do you, yourself, experience your emotions strongly?
- Do you find yourself knowing, doing or seeing things you later realize someone close to you or someone you follow was doing?
- Do you tend to be "disorganized"? In here I mean in physical cleanliness and order, not if you know where everything is, despite the mess.
- Do you find yourself making decisions based on connections that seem disparate?
- Do you get confused when you try to explain your reasoning to someone else when they ask how did you know/do that?
- Has it happened to you that you become overwhelmed in social situations to the point of discomfort and need to leave?
- Do you find it easier sometimes to explain things in metaphors or relate it to a different sense organ?
- Do you have a tendency to addiction, obession or avoidance?
- Is it hard for you to do things when you don't feel like it?
- Do people tell you that you are chaotic in your ways but you think it's others' who are too rigid?
- Do you see or used to see as a kid spirits/ghosts or other disembodied beings that others couldn't?
- Do you feel plants and animals "speak" in a subtle way?
- Do you feel embarrassed regularly after you said or did something while you were exalted?

Of course, this is just a set of questions to trigger a way of thinking about yourself, not a "personality test", so it is not the answer to them but the process of questioning that matters.

If someone is eager in taking magical training but thinks they might be psychic or sensitive, the good news is, you are probably going to be good at it, but you will have to wait on the good parts a bit, and that will be hard because its where you will probably feel comfortable with and is why you came to magic in the first place.

What we need is an emphasis on structure, because we probably lack it in comparison, but it is not an imposed and rigid structure what we need, but one that allows you to have a center that is always there to come back to. It is not about becoming a structured person, I really can't do that, it is about having enough structure so that you can move your psychism farther without its intensity sweeping up your feet, because that is what structure (and patience) is for the mind, a way of instilling into it the elemental Earth qualities and this is very important to have. Just like in taichi/qigong (well, the sample week I took of it) every time they move their energies out in front or above, they touch ground again, one way or the other, we need to do the same thing when we move our minds.

To me this is the hardest because it is in highly volatile situations that I can put my psychism to best use, I can see and intuit better than most people, so I thrive when what is required is quick thinking and attention to the moving things in front of me more than on habit, discipline or knowledge. On the contrary, on a context that requires structure and discipline I feel like a fish out of the water. The way to think about it is this: most people find the meditation and scrying parts of training foreign and incredibly boring; slow and not knowing what to do with them besides slogging through them and takes at least a few weeks or months to see results. It is likely those are your favorite practices; they come naturally and feel familiar and perhaps you already have your own techniques but didn't know that's what it was. On the contrary, journaling, a routine, planning, and finishing things seems incredibly hard, tedious and a way to take all of the fun out of the process, when for other people that part is easy and is what eventually gives them the confirmation that magic is real and takes them further into the work. Well, just as people with those skills need to train their minds in sensitiveness, we need to train our minds in resilience, strength and most importantly, how to regulate it, because we are unconsciously used to tune into something, letting it move with the environment and then reading that movement as intuitions. To gain control of your psychism with structure is power --for as much as it feels like it will bring you boredom and death (it only will feel like that if you try to become a structured person instead of just adding a little more structure at a time).

So, timing, a little planning and routine, exercise that makes you sweat and good daily habits, heck, basically anything that brings a stable daily-life, need to be taken as seriously as the spiritual practices. At least for me, it was really odd to understand that to engage safely and powerfully in magic you need to get good at the mundane, and especially if you are sensitive. But it really does, in fact, if you do the above, the farther you get into magical progress the more in control your psychism will be; the more you will want it to shut down and at the same time the mundane feels amazing and those little things you hated now are the place you come to rest to and recover for another trip into the mysteries. It is almost as if there is a karmic-need to perceive the subtle so the only choice you really have is to perceive it in a chaotic way as life throws it at you or to take control of it and make it happen how you want it to happen for a life in equilibrium.

Nice! I like that. So my first bit of context had a synchronicity in it, and now that you mention it, I do identify a bit with Ariel on The Book of Haatan at this point in my life too.

That makes a lot of sense to see it within the four elements, it seems to me it would be a good idea to have or create a system similar to hatha yoga, but for the mind instead of the body --for similar reasons-- that takes into account "body types" or so based on western philosophy. The OSA works that on the emotional side, but the structure side of daily life is something I have not seen at all discussed besides saying "yup, keep it up, its important" but to me that felt like saying to someone "yup, keep reading the birds, its important" (So, its like when you see a bird doing a particular thing when you are thinking about something specific right? I said once to a friend).

There was nothing there to "keep up". It makes sense though, magic is not supposed to teach you that and I might just be a bad case of it, all the people I know have a sense for those things, but I grew up in a house with two medical doctors on the hospital all day and a huge natural area with iguanas to chase and tons of plants, tress and bugs basically in my backyard, like Mowgli. So, yeah, calendars weren't a thing, not even in college. I did very good in school, but I never planned for it, the structure was there for me already! When to study (class time or when a hot girl needed it) and when to turn in stuff (the day before, unless the hot girl planned ahead) and if I needed more time I could distract the teacher asking her something interesting about her life or her class! (I was popular that year, once someone asked me, can you do that thing you do with Nina today? I didn't study for the history quiz).

Fortune mentions something similar on her essays as well as Butler; and JMG mentions it often here and on his books; and Jorge also told me, but myself not knowing what a structure was I had no idea what that meant! It was by reading a book from Dolores-Ashcroft(she is very psychic) and Herbie Brennan, in the way she talked about it not what she said, that I started to get it. Keeping regular hours, instilling a habit of patience (would you believe a seed taught me that?), cleanliness and functional order and exercise works for me in ways I thought I was only going to be able to do when I was really good at magic. Pleasant surprise! It's much easier and right in front me. Thanks again, Saturn!

As for Water (I would love to hear your perspective some time for the order, motivation and discipline side of things) I would say perhaps more funky free-form art making and watching, or privately dancing to jazz at unscheduled hours? For example, I took a class by Giovanni Zapulla, a Qigong master and contemporary dancer where he gave us an exercise that was really interesting called "statues". You would go into a bodily position and make a face without moving, the first that comes; feel it, and then, without thinking, you would "dance" that position+face out to land in another "statue" and repeating for a whole dance session. I described it as divining/journaling with the body. You tune into something (the emotion in the body+face, because there is an energy with every body position, that's why asanas work) and then let it move you (the dance) and that let's you express repressed things in the movement. Here is a video of a play Giovanni directed --for as much as I don't like it as a performance art that much (it's too abstract for me) it's fascinating to do-- the dancing out the statue part looks like those unstructured movements. If you watch carefully a few minutes you can see how the dancers start to sync up, that's not entirely rehearsed. I bring this up as a source of inspiration in case that gives you an idea!

For what is worth, I once gave a reading "as it came out", whatever it was, with someone I trust, no censoring (I said very funny things). But I learned that I was more intuitive that I thought! And the reading you gave to me was spot on last time, btw.

comments.title: Saturn and the cannabis plant
comments.content:
Hello Mr Greer!

I have some MM-questions concerning the cannabis plant:

1) I recently learned that the cannabis plant corresponds/is ruled with/by Saturn.
In my understanding Saturn is very much a bastion of traditional mores and a conservative outlook on life, a god that will show people their boundaries in a very no-nonsense way.
To put it bluntly Saturn will tell you "f*** around and find out". How can one square this with the massive use of cannabis in the various western counter-cultures and anti-establishment types from the late 50ies on?

2) In connection with this I think that the use of marihuana as a pharmakon for various chronical illnesses might be related to the Saturn connection.
What would the effects of a spagyric tincture of cannabis be? A medicine for chronic issues or something that will turn people terribly stoned? Or both?

Thank you for your advice!

replies.title: Re: Saturn and the cannabis plant
replies.content:
1) Saturn is a much more complex planet than that! He's also the planet of rest ("Saturday, takin' it easy...") and the Lord of the Golden Age. The role of Saturnian cannabis in post-1950s countercultures is easy to understand, though, if you remember that liberalism became the establishment ideology after the Second World War. Thus there's always been a deep though usually unrecognized conservatism in those same countercultures -- think of the hippies going back to the land and taking up folk music.

2) Until you can answer that question for yourself, on the basis of a good solid knowledge of the effect of spagyrics, I don't recommend experimenting.

comments.title: Golden Age, Shambhala
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

Has there ever been a "Golden Age" or utopian society on earth, either regionally or globally? Will there ever be in humanity's future? Do you subscribe to the hope that the full flowering of tbe Aquarian age will witness a renewed, more peaceful and brighter world?

And what are your thoughts about the existence of Shambhala, where it is claimed Madame Blavatsky traveled and encountered Eastern Masters of the Wisdom at their abodes?

Thank you!

Anthony from Pittsburgh PA

replies.title: Re: Golden Age, Shambhala
replies.content:
1) No, there hasn't, and there won't be. "Of the crooked timber of humanity was no straight thing ever made." As the saying goes, humans gonna human, and pretending that we can be better than we are is a good recipe for disaster. As for the Aquarian age, I really wish the hippies had laid off the weed a bit more. The age of Aquarius will not bring "a renewed, more peaceful and brighter world," any more than the age of Pisces did -- yes, those same fantasies were in circulation two thousand years ago. The Aquarian age will have different problems, but it'll still have plenty of problems.

We will each experience a better world only when each of us individually earns the right to such a world. To earn that, it's necessary to deal with your karma and your share of humanity's collective karma, to awaken into self-knowledge and self-mastery, and to use the resulting gifts in harmony with the Divine. That's how each of us can graduate from the human level and go on to more interesting things.

2) Shambhala is a spiritual reality, not a physical one. There are places on Earth where it's easier to contact that reality, and not all of them are in the Himalayas; Mount Shasta in California is such a place, for example.

replies.title: Re: Golden Age, Shambhala
replies.content: Mount Shasta, halfway up at the ski bowl in the summer, was where I had my first migraine aura.  Back around 1988, and periodically ever since.  I guess that's not exactly something to celebrate.

replies.title: Re: Golden Age, Shambhala
replies.content: It's a place of wild magic, and the energies can be very rough.

replies.title: Re: Golden Age, Shambhala
replies.content: Do you think Blavatsky's visit to Shambhala, as conveyed in the recently published book "Kingdom of Gods" purportedly parsed from her own diaries, enountered one of these "portals" that bridged the physical to higher realms, i.e. that it was indeed an authentic experience of Shambhala via one of these access points?

replies.title: Re: Golden Age, Shambhala
replies.content: I have no idea. First, I haven't read the account, and second, judging the nature and validity of another person's spiritual experiences is a very challenging task best left to saints, of which I'm emphatically not one.

replies.title: Re: Dog dreams

comments.title: It's the Nature Report
comments.content:
Most of the wildlife has shaken off winter and is fully up and running. The weather has been kind to the blueberry bush, I'm going to have to fob them off on relatives come June. The only real anomaly are the thistles - they are all blooming about 3 weeks early. Some of them are even "fluffing out", they've gone that far.

The wasps are busy building nests in places where they shouldn't, the carpenter bees are out looking for holes to drill but their little insect hearts don't seem to be in it this year. It's like they're just going through the motions. Still, down they come with a good spray. An early spray keeps the bugs away. The bugs that seems to have any liveliness are the ants. Which reminds me, this year I go nuclear on the ants.

Usually I'd mark the beginning of summer with the thistles all in bloom but it's way too early for that. I guess it'll have to be when I start having to mow every week instead of every other.

Summer is kind of boring for nature watching - not much changes until you get to the end when the plants pick up on the shorter days. So that's probably when I do another one of these.

Hi John,

I hope that you are well.

I recently read a news article that made reference to a transcription of a conversation between J R R Tolkien and C.S Lewis in which Tolkien gave an opinion that I, as an Irishman find startling and strangely credible.

Tolkien described Ireland as 'naturally evil'. He could 'feel evil coming up from under the earth, from the peat bogs, from the clumps of trees, even from the cliffs, and this evil was only held in check by the great devotion of the southern Irish to their religion' Perilous Realms p.19.

Regards
Dermotok

replies.title: Re: J R R Tolkien on Ireland
replies.content:
I found a comment on Twitter yesterday suggesting that Tolkien drew inspiration for Sauron, and the imagery of the Lidless Eye, from an Irish legend of Balor of the Evil Eye.

One comment there mentioned a letter by Tolkien, dated 1937, in response to a reviewer comparing his books to Celtic myth:

Needless to say they are not Celtic! Neither are the tales. I do know Celtic things (many in their original languages Irish and Welsh), and feel for them a certain distaste: largely for their fundamental unreason. They have bright colour, but are like a broken stained glass window reassembled without design. They are in fact ‘mad’ as your reader says—but I don’t believe I am.

The suggestion was made that this could be connected to Tolkien's vision of a great deluge, which led him to create Numenor, his mythic version of Atlantis.

I know precious little of Irish or Celtic legend, so I have no more to add, but it would fit with this theory.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I could get myself into a world of trouble by answering that honestly...

Sigh. Here goes.

The Neopagan movement is in deep spiritual trouble. Until 1980 or so, Wicca was a private initiatory tradition with its own relationship to specific gods and goddesses -- "the God" and "the Goddess" were labels used to guard the secret names of the deities in question, which varied from tradition to tradition. Once the 1980s dawned, though, Starhawk, Margot Adler, and a flurry of others redefined Wicca as a replacement for the dead counterculture of the 1960s, in which women's empowerment, casual sex, and various playacting habits borrowed from the Society for Creative Anachronism replaced serious magical or spiritual practice.

Crucial to that was the transformation of "the Goddess" into a vague label for femininity in general, redefined as divine. Recall those bumper stickers that read, "Back off, I'm a goddess"? That was indicative. What happened was that the new pop-culture Neopagans stopped worshipping the gods and started worshipping an abstract image of themselves instead. That's a very risky habit, and what usually occurs is the same thing that happened to the Spiritualist movement once it fell into the same trap: moral collapse, followed by a descent into increasingly grubby magical practices, and the final disintegration of the movement. I talked about that here:

https://ecosophia.dreamwidth.org/39410.html

And here:

https://www.ecosophia.net/the-flight-to-the-fringes-and-what-waits-there/

There are small-p pagan groups that haven't fallen into that trap -- some Heathen groups, some old-fashioned Druid groups, and a few others -- but not many of them. I'm not sure it's going to be possible to save much of anything from the impending wreck of the Neopagan movement, and I'd be wary of the group you interact with -- that energetic grubbiness may turn into moral squalor, in ways that could hurt your kids.

Russia Paints New Military Insignia on Invading Force - Same Symbol Used in Past Wars against . . . Khazaria

Russia has launched a new offensive into northeastern Ukraine, aimed at Kharkiv and Sumy regions. But the HUGE news about this isn't the invasion itself, it's the SYMBOL Russia is painting on this new force: ‘Rune of Gungnir’ -- the spear of Odin.

https://halturnerradioshow.com/index.php/news-selections/world-news/russia-paints-new-military-insignia-on-invading-force-same-symbol-used-in-past-wars-against-khazaria

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
A couple things worth pointing out here:

1) Khazaria was/is famous for being a central Asian steppe nomad kingdom that adopted Judaism, so I can't help but read the linked articles quote about the Russians "knowing EXACTLY who they're fighting" to mean "the Jews" - take that as you will in interpreting the article. Btw, speaking of Khazaria, Michael Chabon has a fun little pulp-style swashbuckling story reminiscent of Fritz Lieber's Fafhrd and the Gray Mouser, without the magic, set in Khazaria called Gentlemen of the Road.

2) Within the Elder, Younger, and Anglo-Saxon Futharks, there is no Rune for "Gungnir," and none that exactly match that shape. Some versions of the rune "Gar" from the Anglo-Saxon Futhorc, which does, in fact, mean "spear" look like that shape, but the Anglo-Saxon Futhorc would have been extremely unlikely to have anything to do with the Kievan Rus, the Varangians, or the other Scandinavians running around in early Russia/Ukraine/Central Asia, and by the time they were doing their thing, the Anglo-Saxons had been Christian for a century or two anyway.

All of which is to say a square with an X through it is not a particularly unusual symbol to come up with for military identification and/or decoration, whether ancient or modern. See, for example the NATO Joint Military Symbology, which is a way to represent units and their characteristics on maps in an iconic way that has its roots in Napoleon's general staff: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/NATO_Joint_Military_Symbology

Now, is it possible that the Russians are tapping into an old symbol on purpose? And that it might have some significance to Woden/Odin? Quite possibly, but it doesn't strike me as a slam dunk from just those pictures.

Cheers,
Jeff

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Yes, I saw that. Whether this symbol...
[IMAGE:0]
...was traditionally associated with Gungnir or not, there's a lot of chatter online associating the two. Since Russian culture absorbed a strong Viking influence in its early days, and Russia increasingly sees itself as the defender of European tradition against arrogant globalism, it wouldn't surprise me at all if the choice was deliberate -- and that's very troubling, considering which nation last defined itself as the defender of Europe and invoked Odin in that context...

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
There is absolutely zero extant historical evidence about how the Pagan Varangian warriors of Svyatoslav and the other early Russian princes marked their armor. (These early Pagan Varangians were Scandinavians, mostly from what is now Sweden, who had seized rulership over what is now Ukraine and Western Russia.) Nor were these Varangians called bogatyrs back in those early days.

There are indeed a few ancient Western European spearheads and swords with short Runic inscriptions, but these seem mostly to be names of makers, of owners, or of the weapons themselves, and some seem to be intended to as magical enhancements of the weapons' destructive power.

There is no old source that identifies the symbol on the vehicle as either an individual rune or as a symbol for "Gugnir."

The white symbol imposed over the left front corner of the vehicle says in Russian "North Wind" above the bear and "Fearless ones" below the bear; beween the lower Russian phrase and the bear is "Warrior of North" in English. The truck might be Russian, for all I can tell, but the photograph as given here is an obvious manipulation for by an English speaker.

To the laft of the blown-up image of the "gugnir" symbol is a chart of symbols with Russian interpretations. The Russian label next to the "gugnir" symbol and the one below it translates as "plowed earth." The label on next symbol down, with two "gugnir" symbols inside a rectangle, means "plowed earth, warmed by the sun." And the symbol below that is labeled "sown field" (that is, sown with seed). They have nothing whatever to do with war and warriors, let alone Varangians.

The Khazars were originally a Turkic people, who eventually converted to Judaism. The Pagan Varangians would hardly have cared about the Khazars' religion, one way or another. That's a modern thing.

As an account of either real history or current events, the whole post reeks to high heaven of a hog wallow.

Then there is Hal Turner himself, on whom Wikipedia has a fairly long and relevant article ...

comments.title: Allegies and the Esoteric
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

I am hard at work developing my meditation practice as a beginner esotericist but particularly in this dreadful season, I find myself hitting a serious stumbling block: my rather severe allergies, that are not only a general problem every Spring for me, but really love to manifest while I am meditating and trying to have nice, clear measured breathing. I find myself often being forced to breathe through the mouth, and must deal with sneezing and runny nose through most sessions. Techniques like alternate nostril breathing are right out.

Surely I am not alone in this? Do you or others here struggle with this, and do you have strategies for how to deal with it?

replies.title: Re: Allegies and the Esoteric
replies.content: That sort of thing is common, because the subconscious is a creature of habit and doesn't like the change that comes with regular meditation. It'll throw all kinds of fits to try to distract you! Just keep on doing the practices, breathe through your mouth as necessary, and put up with the sneezing and the runny nose. Once your subconscious gets used to meditation, it'll stop -- I know people with allergies, in fact, whose noses clear when they meditate.

replies.title: Re: Allegies and the Esoteric
replies.content:
On the more material side, you might consider nasal rinses of one kind or another (I suspect that neti pots were literally invented to assist with Pranayama, but I don't actually know that). Some folks find squirt bottles convenient, others full-on neti pots. I bought a little nasal spray bottle with an atomizer on top and mix up my own saline solution to put in it (unfortunately, only good for ~3 days if you want to be on the safe side). If I notice symptoms early and do a few spritzes of that a day, I keep most of the bad stuff under control, usually. If it gets to actual congestion, a neti pot can be helpful.

Anyhow, they've helped me tremendously despite a lifetime of nasal/sinus trouble of one sort of another.

Cheers,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Allegies and the Esoteric
replies.content: Please use distilled or boiled water with either neti pots or squirt bottles, though! Tap water, even when safe for drinking, can contain amoebas and bacteria: https://phelpshealth.org/news/featured-stories/dos-and-donts-using-neti-pots-and-sinus-rinses. That said, nasal rinses can be very helpful for nasal congestion - the squirt bottles from the drugstore work well for me.

replies.title: Re: Allegies and the Esoteric
replies.content: Yes, sorry, forgot to mention that I always boil the water first!

replies.title: Re: Themes for discursive meditation
replies.content:
It's really a matter of finding some text, or some set of images, that appeals to you, and then making a commitment to go all the way through it. If you're like most people you'll get bored halfway through, and want to go to something else! Don't do it -- just keep on with the text you've chosen, and you'll find interest returning soon enough.

replies.title: Re: Themes for discursive meditation
replies.content:
Some other options you might consider:

- A collection of mythology you find appealing (The Prose Edda is great for Norse myth, Edith Hamilton for Greek, the Mabinogion would complement your prior Druidical engagement)
- A work of philosophy you find meaningful (Marcus Aurelius's Meditations or Publius Syrus's aphorisms are nicely bite sized, if Stoicism suits you)
- A set of divinatory symbols you want to get to know well enough to use (possibly also too "out there," but you can always tell yourself it's just symbolism - Tarot, Ogham, Runes, Sacred Geometry Oracle)
- A traditional system of symbols (Four elements, 12 signs of the Zodiac, etc)
- Something more pictorial, like one of the alchemical works mentioned in JMG's latest post on The Ritual of High Magic

Whatever works for you, good luck!
Jeff

My readings so far reveal that when introducing Mercury, Mr. Leo wrote "...according to a certain body of occult teaching this planet is destined to become the future physical home for the majority of our humanity". Also Venus is currently inhabited and "her humanity has reached a very high stage". I've read that souls will go to other planets but not material plane humans... Is this from Theosophy?

In one of the book's appendixes Leo writes that besides the well known branches of astrology there is also Atmospheric or Meteorological astrology; Medical astrology and Esoteric or Metaphysical astrology which, I think, is a counterpart of Vedic astrology (i.e. concerned with the soul). First time I came across the idea that astrology could be used for weather forecasting.

replies.title: Re: Alan Leo
replies.content:
Good heavens, yes. Alan Leo, real name Frederick W. Allan, was the most important astrologer in the English-speaking world at the turn of the last century and you can learn a lot from his books. They're all in the public domain these days, btw!

Astrology used to be used fairly often for weather predicting, as it was better than most of what was available before scientific meteorology took off; I don't know of anybody using it these days, though.

comments.title: praying for others and the law of silence
comments.content:
I've question about the Law of Silence and praying for others.

The rule of keeping quiet about magical working makes sense.

However, when I am praying for others, other than the most general feelings of good-will, I want them to know. Part of that is just the common courtesy - and, to me, another spiritual law - that I do not pray for a person without their consent.

The other is that I want them to know I am praying for them, as that is itself a gesture of caring, and their knowing that may make them more receptive, and they may be comforted by that.

Past that initial consent, though, I just shut up and do the work, and I don't discuss that with others.

Do we have some complementary guiding principles here, or is there a contradiction I'm missing?

replies.title: Re: praying for others and the law of silence
replies.content: That seems like a reasonable approach. It's important to have consent, of course, and the caring expressed in "May I pray for you?" is also important. At the same time, Matthew 5:5-6 is relevant here, of course -- and also suggests that the guy from Nazareth knew his way around the magical virtues...

replies.title: Re: praying for others and the law of silence
replies.content:
I think I got this take from Jordan Peterson initially, but I've taken one important piece of "not praying in public" to be as a protection against the other motives and rewards that can creep in for "being seen to pray" - others admiring your piety/care/devotion, demonstrating what you believe publicly, inviting punishment that you can bravely bear as a martyr, and so forth.

That all makes sense to me, and I wonder if it might be part of what's behind the magical virtue of keeping silent as well.

Cheers,
Jeff

comments.title: Reincarnation and planetary talismans
comments.content:
As always, thank you for this tremendous resource. It is invaluable.

1: A question regarding incarnation, how long do you think it will take for the "surplus of human bodies" problem to allow us to cycle in-and-out of incarnation more healthily? I ask because you at one point suggested that Paschal Beverly Randolph might have been incarnated as Aleister Crowley, and since Dr. Randolph died on July 29, 1875 and Crowley incarnated on October 12th of that same year, my question to you is:

Do you believe that this state of "immediately cycled back in" was going that strongly by 1875? If that's the case, since at the time the world population was roughly 1.3 billion, are we going to be stuck in this situation for a good long while yet? Personally, few things you have written have resonated with me as much as this idea that we're all frazzled souls in strong need of rest.

To say nothing of the effects it's having re: gender dysphoria. I am beginning to feel as if many trans people, transwomen in particular, seem to be - if not outright possessed - definitely obsessed by some lowe-astral entities, "futa" or otherwise. What are the effects of that mass possession/obsession, long term? Where is this going?

My second question is more to do with planetary magic:

Can the energies of Saturn be used to consecrate a protective talisman? I know Saturn is known as the "Greater Malefic" but also of boundaries, limits, consequences, and a more grounded, "adult" feeling rather than the more aggressive and freewheeling feeling of Mars. At the very least, that's how the two planets have always felt to me, and it turned out that Saturn is very well dignified in my chart. Would this be possible? Are there any pitfalls to watch out for?

Thank you again. This turned into more like three questions, too!

replies.title: Re: Reincarnation and planetary talismans
replies.content:
1) That was something that happened with some people even when the human population was much smaller. In Randolph's case, he died suddenly by violence, and very likely felt his work was only half-done, so he may have made an effort to get back into incarnation promptly. I'm guessing we have maybe the rest of the century to get through before the population drops low enough that this is only a very occasional thing.

2) These kinds of mass crazes tend to peak and collapse in relatively short order, all things considered. I expect it to wind down over the next decade or so, leaving a lot of mutilated and drug-injured people behind.

3) Yes, you can do this, but it's important to realize what you're letting yourself in for. Saturn is the lord of strict justice, and he will not protect you from something that you've brought on yourself. He will also expect you to keep your promises and respect boundaries to a greater extent than you may find comfortable...

replies.title: Re: Reincarnation and planetary talismans
replies.content: I can confirm 3) and want to add that it doesn't even need formal ritual or magic for Saturn taking you up on your offer. I get my behind kicked hard every time I slack off (though considering the lunar mess I habitually am, I do feel he's taking that into account and gives me more leeway than he'd do for someone with a stronger Saturn placement or pronounced Capricorn in their natal chart), and I won't lie, there are days when I feel very, very tired and think to myself, "0/10, wouldn't recommend, wouldn't do this again."

replies.title: Re: Contemplation & Sacred Geometry
